evidence_quotes.quote: Recommendation: The Digital Fairness Act should include clear measures guaranteeing continued access to purchased digital content, regardless of post-sale decisions by the publisher or similar.

Recommendation: The Act should affirm that platforms and payment providers must remain neutral regarding legal content and products and only restrict access in accordance with clearly established laws.
Age verification must be privacy-preserving, decentralized, and minimally invasive; biometric or centralized methods should be avoided.

paragraphs.text: I would like to thank the Commission for the opportunity to provide feedback on the proposed Digital Fairness Act. As a digital consumer and supporter of the Stop Killing Games initiative, I support the increase to fairness and transparency in digital markets. I would also like to submit concerns to consumer rights, protections, digital content and data privacy that deserve further attention from this proposal. 1. Ensuring Continued Access to Purchased Digital Products One of the primary concerns in todays digital world is the loss of access to legally purchased digital products. For example, Ubisofts Video-game "The Crew" was rendered unplayable in 2024 following the shutdown of its online servers for users who had paid for it and even for those who chose to play single-player which should've been offline. Consumers should be able to trust that when they buy a digital product, they can will be able to use it, even after a platform, developer or publisher stops supporting it. Recommendation: The Digital Fairness Act should include clear measures guaranteeing continued access to purchased digital content, regardless of post-sale decisions by the publisher or similar. These protections should also apply to previously purchased content. 2. Preventing Ideologically and Commercially Driven Content Removal by Third Parties There have been cases of advocacy organizations pressuring platforms and payment service providers to restrict or remove access to digital content based on ideological rather then legal ground. a notable example of which is a campaign by the group Collective Shout in Australia, which has led to the delisting of games perceived as offensive. Outside of ideological pressure, this sets a precedent where payment providers may be financially incentivized or pressured to restrict certain sellers or products, limiting competition and consumer choice. For example, a payment processor could be paid to block competing companies and only allow favored companies to operate in the market. Such practices set a very dangerous precedent where legal products can be suppressed or excluded based on subjective values and/or financial arrangements, bypassing any legal and democratic oversight. Recommendation: The Act should affirm that platforms and payment providers must remain neutral regarding legal content and products and only restrict access in accordance with clearly established laws. 3. Protecting Privacy: Age Verification and Risks of Backdoors While protecting people especially minors online is a important goal, Systems and proposals requiring biometric data, government ID uploads, or centralized databases raise serious privacy and security concerns such as data breaches, misuse, and exclusion of users without official documents. Moreover, mandates for software backdoors allowing government access to private communications are alarming and will weaken cybersecurity, erode trust, and create opportunities for abuse and hacking. Recommendations: Age verification must be privacy-preserving, decentralized, and minimally invasive; biometric or centralized methods should be avoided. The Act should explicitly oppose mandatory backdoors or government-mandated access points in digital products, ensuring privacy and security by design. 4. Recognising and Protecting Digital Ownership Currently, digital goods are often licensed rather than sold outright, because of that legal consumers may lose access to content due to DRM restrictions, online-only requirements, or server shutdowns, even for single-player games. This undermines consumer expectations and creates a false sense of ownership. Recommendation: The Act should affirm the principle of digital ownership, including the right to access and use purchased products long-term. Where technically feasible, consumers should be provided with backup options or offline-accessible versions of content in the event of service discontinuation.
